segments.s067-g145: پناه گرفتن + به: the idiom 'پناه گرفتن به (کسی/چیزی)' means 'to take refuge in (someone/something)'. Here the preposition به introduces یزدان ('in/with God'). گرفتند is past tense, 3rd person plural ('they took'), and هر دو ('both') indicates that two people/things are the ones taking refuge; the word order is inverted/poetic (one could expect 'هر دو پناه به یزدان گرفتند' or 'پناه هر دو به یزدان گرفت') but the meaning is the same.
segments.s067-g146: هم ... و هم ... is a correlative pair meaning 'both ... and ...'. این points to the immediately mentioned item ('this'), دل‌شده is a participial/adjectival compound meaning 'love-struck' or 'smitten', and ماه is used metaphorically here as 'the beautiful one' (a common Persian epithet). the second هم before پیشگاه repeats the correlative 'and also', and پیشگاه means 'the (royal) presence/court' — together the phrase lists the two who sought refuge.
segments.s067-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past tense 'he came'. ز + درگاه is the prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold (presence) of...', and مهراب is the name (here the court's owner) following درگاه. شاد postposes the predicate and qualifies the subject as 'glad/joyful' — idiomatically 'he came glad(ly)'.
segments.s067-g2: همی is the archaic continuous/progressive particle meaning 'was continually / kept on'. کرد is the past 'did' used here as the light verb for a verbal phrase. از introduces the object/topic of remembrance ('of/about Zal'). زال is the proper name Zal. بسیار modifies یاد and here means 'much/greatly' (i.e. he remembered him intensely). یاد is the noun 'remembrance/ memory' — together همی کرد یاد = 'kept remembering'.
segments.s067-g3: گرانمایه is an honorific adjective 'precious/esteemed'. سیندخت is the feminine proper name (object). را is the Persian definite/object marker marking سیندخت as the direct object. خفته is a predicative/adjectival past participle 'asleep'. دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw'. Word order is verb-final in the clause-after-object style common in poetry.
segments.s067-g4: رخش here denotes 'her countenance/face' rather than the horse (contextually the woman's looks). پژمریده is an adjectival participle 'withered/pale'. دل‌آشفته is a compound adjective 'heart-distraught' = 'distraught'. دید repeats the past verb 'he saw' applying to these descriptive observations. The clause lists multiple predicates seen by the subject.
segments.s067-g5: بپرسید is the simple past 'he asked'. و is the coordinating 'and'. گفتا is an archaic past form 'he said'. چه بودت is an idiomatic past construction: چه 'what' + بودت (بود + enclitic ت 'to/for you') = 'what was to you / what has happened to you'. بگوی here is an imperative/command 'tell (me)' or a hortative 'say', though the listed lemmas portray conversational senses.
segments.s067-g6: چرا is 'why'. پژمرید is a past/adjectival form 'withered/wan'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. چو is the comparative/simile 'as/like'. گلبرگ روی is a simile: گلبرگ 'petal' modifying روی 'face' (here 'your face like a petal'). The clause asks why she has become pale/withering, comparing her face to a petal.
segments.s067-g7: چنین 'thus/so' opens the reply. داد is the past 'gave' used with پاسخ (answer) to mean 'gave an answer' — together 'چنین داد پاسخ' = 'thus he answered'. به introduces the indirect object 'to Mehrab'. باز here means 'back' (i.e. replied/returned the answer).
segments.s067-g8: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. اندیشه is 'a thought'. اندر is the archaic preposition 'in/within'. دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive ـم). شد is the past of 'become' (here 'became/got'). دراز is the adjective 'long' — together 'that a thought in my heart became long' i.e. 'a long/lingering thought came into my heart.' Note: the tokenization separates the possessive suffix from دل; the suffix is functioning as 'my'.
segments.s067-g57: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. این is the demonstrative 'this' (referring to the foregoing); دید is the past tense 'saw' (perfect). The clause then continues with the proper name سیندخت as the subject and the compound verbal phrase برپای جست 'sprang to her feet' (برپای is an adverbial/locative phrase 'upright / to her feet' and جست is the past of جستن 'leapt').
segments.s067-g58: کمر کرد is a light-verbal construction: کمر (literally 'waist' / 'girdle') + کرد (past 'did/made') gives the sense 'girded' or 'wound (around)'; here it idiomatically means she put/wrapped. بر introduces the location 'on/upon'; گردگاهش 'his/her neck' (گردگاه + -ش possessive). دو دست 'two hands' follows the locative, so the whole phrase means 'she put/wrapped her two hands about his neck.'
segments.s067-g59: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'she said thus'. کز here functions as 'from'/'that comes from' (introducing source: 'from the younger'); کهتر means 'the younger' or 'inferior/lesser'. اکنون یکی = 'now one' i.e. 'now, one thing' or 'now, hear one (my) word' — the phrase prepares the imperative that follows.
segments.s067-g60: سخن بشنو is an imperative: سخن 'speech/word' as object and بشنو 'listen!' (imperative of شنیدن). و links the next command; گوش دار اندکی is another hortative: گوش دار literally 'hold ear' i.e. 'pay attention' (گوش here stands for attention) and اندکی 'a little' modifies it. Together: 'Listen to the word, and pay attention a little.'
segments.s067-g61: ازان پس = 'after that' (از + آن + پس). همان کن is an imperative 'do the same' (همان 'that same' + کن 'do!'). که introduces the relative clause 'that/which'; رای آیدت literally 'a counsel/opinion comes to you' — idiomatically 'that which your judgment/plan suggests.' آیدت is آید + enclitic ت (to you).
segments.s067-g62: This short clause lists two nouns joined by و: روان و خرد 'soul and wisdom'. رهنمای is used as 'guide' (apposed/predicative), and آیدت repeats the verb 'comes to you' with the enclitic -ت; overall: 'may soul and wisdom come as your guides' (the verb applies to both preceding nouns).
segments.s067-g63: بپیچید و بنداخت are two past actions 'they wound/encircled' and 'they threw/put'. او را is the definite object 'her' (او + object marker را). بدست here means 'into the hand(s)/into custody' (literally 'to the hand'), so the clause says they bound/turned her and cast her into (someone's) hands.
segments.s067-g64: خروشی برآورد is 'they raised/made a commotion' (خروشی 'a roar/uproar' as object, برآورد past 'brought forth/raised'). چون introduces a simile 'like'; پیل مست 'a drunken elephant' modifies the manner of the uproar (پیل 'elephant', مست 'drunk' used figuratively).
segments.s067-g73: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). سام یل is a name plus epithet (name before epithet is common in poetic word order). با + منوچهر شاه means 'with Manuchehr, the king' (با here = 'together with' / 'alongside'). The whole segment sets up the conditional agents: 'if Sam the hero with Manuchehr the king...'.
segments.s067-g74: بیابند is a 3rd-person plural form (here used optative/conditional: 'they should/shall bring/assemble' or 'they come/arrive'); بر ما = 'against us' (بر used in the sense 'against/toward'); یکی دستگاه = 'one/ a single دستگاه' where دستگاه = 'organization/formation' (here: an army or single force). The verb governs the following prepositional phrase and noun object.
segments.s067-g75: ز کابل is a circumstantial prepositional phrase 'from Kabul'. برآید is a 3rd‑person singular verb meaning 'rises/ascends' (verb‑before‑subject order is common in poetic Persian). به خورشید دود places the subject (دود, 'smoke') with a goal/extent marker به خورشید — 'up to the sun' (literal hyperbolic image).
segments.s067-g76: نه ... نه is the standard 'neither ... nor' coordination. آباد ماند uses آباد (prosperous/inhabited) as predicate adjective and ماند (past tense of ماندن) 'remained/was left' — together 'no inhabited place remained'. نه کشت و درود lists what is gone: 'nor crops (کشت) and درود'. درود here is archaic/poetic and contextually means planted groves/vegetation; it does not match the modern-sense options given, so it's marked below as unmatched.
segments.s067-g77: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'); here گفت functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (verb-first order is normal in poetry). سیندخت is the speaker (proper name). با + مرزبان means 'to/with the marzban' — با in addresses often marks the addressee. Note: the entry for گفت in the supplied senses lists noun meanings; here گفت is the verb 'said' (no supplied sense matches).
segments.s067-g78: کزین = 'from/because of this' introduces the reason. در here is best read as 'door/gate' (در = gate) in this context. مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn/return' (م- negative + گردان(imperative) = 'do not turn away'). به خیره زبان is an adverbial phrase 'with an astonished/staring tongue' = 'with a gaping/astounded utterance' (زبان = speech/utterance). The whole clause is an admonition: 'do not, for this reason, turn away at the gate with astonished speech.'
segments.s067-g79: کزین 'from/thereupon' links to the prior statement. آگهی یافت literally 'found/obtained news' — a standard formula meaning 'received the information' (آگهی = news/information; یافت = past 'found/received'). سام سوار is the agent in apposition (name + epithet 'the rider/horseman'). Word order places the verbal phrase before the name for narrative effect.
segments.s067-g80: به دل = 'into the heart' (indicating the internal target). ترس و تیمار و سختی is a triad 'fear and solicitude/care and hardship'. مدار here is a prohibitive/negative imperative construction meaning 'do not hold/let [them] be' (classical Persian م‑ prefix + دار(=hold) = 'do not hold/harbor'). So the clause admonishes: 'do not let fear, care and hardship into your heart.' Note: the supplied senses for مدار are nominal and do not match this verbal use, so its sense is marked as unmatched below.
segments.s067-g129: This is a nominal phrase describing appearance: بهشتی ('heavenly/paradisal') is the head noun/adjective; بد آراسته is a two-word modifier where آراسته is a past-participle adjective 'adorned' and بد here functions as an intensifier/qualifier attached to آراسته (an archaic usage not listed in the provided senses); پرنگار is a postpositive adjective meaning 'richly ornamented/painted'. Together they form one image phrase that will be compared by چو in the next segment.
segments.s067-g130: چو is the comparative/temporal particle 'like/when' introducing a simile. خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective 'the shining sun' (noun first, adjective following). به + خرم بهار expresses location/time 'in the joyful spring' with به here functioning as a preposition ('into/toward' → rendered 'in'), خرم 'joyful/lush' modifies بهار 'spring'.
segments.s067-g131: Temporal clause: پدر 'the father' is the subject. چون (here 'when') introduces the time clause; ورا is the enclitic object 'to her/at her' (vora = 'to her' or 'toward her'); دید is the past tense verb 'saw'. خیره بماند is a small predicate phrase meaning 'was left staring/stood agape' — خیره 'staring/astounded' modifies the verb بماند ('remained/was left'), forming the resultive state after seeing.
segments.s067-g132: جهان‌آفرین 'the World‑Creator/Creator' is the direct object; را is the standard definite object marker attached to it. نهانی is an adverb meaning 'secretly' placed before the verb. بخواند (past narrative third person) here means 'he called/summoned' (opted from available senses as 'to call/summon'), so the clause means 'he secretly summoned the Creator.'
segments.s067-g133: بدو گفت is a ditransitive sequence: بدو 'to her/to him' marks the indirect object and گفت is the reported-speech verb 'said' (the provided senses list does not include the verbal sense, so marked accordingly). ای introduces a vocative 'O!'; شسته مغز 'washed brain' is a compound where شسته (a past participle) modifies مغز 'brain' and the following از خرد ('of wisdom/reason') is an ablative phrase describing what the brain is 'washed of' — an idiom meaning 'deprived of sense.'
segments.s067-g134: ز (poetic variant of از) marks the source/set 'of/from the jeweled ones' where پرگوهران is the plural adjective/noun 'the jeweled/precious ones'. این 'this' points to the person in question; کی is an interrogative 'who/which' here ('which one'); اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'among/in'; خورد is used in the sense 'befits / falls to / is suitable for' (i.e., 'is a match for'), so the clause asks, 'Among these jeweled ones, who is fit for this one?'
segments.s067-g135: که introduces a subordinate/exclamatory clause 'that...'. با is the preposition 'with'; اهرمن is the proper name 'Ahriman/the demon'; جفت here is used as a verb-noun 'to pair/mate' (sense 'mate/spouse') and گردد is the verb 'become' (third person subjunctive/optative/present in narrative form), so the clause says 'that a fairy (پری) should become paired with Ahriman.'
segments.s067-g136: Another optative/exclamatory clause introduced by که: مه 'the moon' is the subject; تاج بادت is an optative phrase where بادت is the old optative particle 'may it be' (so 'may the moon be a crown'); the second مه انگشتری repeats the subject and predicate in parallel (elliptically: 'may the moon be a ring'), a rhetorical expression emphasizing impossibility or absurdity.
segments.s067-g105: بترسید is a past-tense, 3rd-person form meaning “was afraid” and is placed before the subject in this poetic inversion; سیندخت is the subject (the proper name) following the verb. ازان is an archaic, fused form of از + آن meaning “that/of that,” and تیز مرد is a noun phrase “swift/sharp man” (تیز modifying مرد).
segments.s067-g106: که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” او را uses the clitic را to mark the person affected (here ‘her’/‘him’) as the object. ز + درد = “from/with pain” (poetic z for از), اندر آرد is a classical sequence: اندر = “into/within” and آرد (present/past-narrative 3rd sg classical form of آوردن) = “brings/causes,” so the clause says he will/does cause her into pain; به گرد is an adverbial phrase “round/about,” completing the image. Word order and classical verbal form reflect Ferdowsi’s elevated style.
segments.s067-g107: بدو گفت is an idiom meaning “he said to her” (بدو = to him/unto her; گفت = ‘said’ here used narratively). پیمانت is composed of پیمان “pledge/covenant” plus the enclitic -ت (ت = your), and خواهم is the 1st‑person present/future auxiliary meaning “I will (have/ask/seek).” نخسْت “first” is an adverb modifying the action.
segments.s067-g108: به چاره is a prepositional phrase “by/with a remedy” (به = by/with; چاره = remedy). دلش را is the object ‘her/his heart’ (دل = heart, ش = possessive suffix ‘his/her’ and را marks it as definite object). ز کینه = “from/of hatred” (ز = from/because of; کینه = hatred), and بشست is a past narrative form of شستن (“he/she washed/cleansed”), so the clause says that (by remedy) he washed her heart of hatred.
segments.s067-g109: زبان داد is an idiom meaning “spoke” (literally ‘gave tongue’); سیندخت را is the indirect object ‘to Sindokht’ marked with را, and نامجوی is the subject name placed at the end (classical word order often puts the subject after the predicate). The overall structure: ‘Namjuy spoke to Sindokht…’.
segments.s067-g110: که introduces reported speech (“that…”). رودابه را is the topical/object-marked proper name Rudabeh. بد here = “bad/ill,” نیارد is a classical/poetic verb form meaning ‘does not bring’ or ‘would not cause’ (negative + root), and بروی (literally ‘to the face/appearance’ or construed as ‘upon her’) completes the idiom ‘بد نیارد بروی’ meaning ‘would not bring disgrace/harm to her appearance.’ The clause thus reassures that Rudabeh will not be a source of disgrace.
segments.s067-g111: بدو گفت again = “he said to her.” بنگر is an imperative ‘look!’ or ‘see!’ addressing the listener. که here begins the content clause that follows (that the king of the realm …). شاه زمین is a genitival/title phrase ‘the king of the land/realm’ where زمین qualifies شاه.
segments.s067-g112: دل از ما کند = ‘will turn his heart away from us’ (دل = heart; از ما = from us; کند is the classical present/future 3rd‑person of کردن ‘to make/do’ used here to mean ‘will turn/withdraw’). زین سخن = ‘because of/from this speech’ (زین is a literary form, سخن = speech), and پر ز کین = ‘full of hatred’ (پر = full (of); ز = of; کین = hatred/vengeance). Word order reflects classical Persian: modifiers follow the nouns they qualify.
segments.s067-g41: 'فرو برد' is a compound past verb: 'فرو' (down-) + 'برد' (past of بردن) = 'brought down / made to bow'. 'سرو سهی' is the noun 'cypress' with the poetic epithet 'سهی' (dark/dusky) following it. 'داد خم' repeats the action with 'داد' (gave, caused) taking 'خم' (a bend/curve) as its object — i.e. 'caused (it) a bend'. The implied subject (he) is omitted, as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s067-g42: 'به' marks the recipient ('to/for'), followed by 'نرگس گل سرخ' = 'the red narcissus-flower' with the object marker 'را' attached for emphasis/definiteness. 'داد' is the past 'gave' and 'نم' (dew/moisture) is what was given; word order (recipient phrase before the verb and the given thing after it) is a common poetic variation. The clause therefore means 'he gave dew to the red narcissus-flower.'
segments.s067-g43: The subordinating 'که' introduces the clause. 'گردون' = 'the sky/heavens (fate)'. 'به سر' = 'upon the head' (an idiom meaning 'to befall someone'), with 'بر' (on/upon) reinforcing the spatial/affective relation, and 'چنان' = 'thus/such'. 'نگذرد' is third-person present/subjunctive/negative form of گذشتن ('to pass') meaning 'may not pass / may not befall / does not happen'. Together: 'that the heavens may not befall us thus.'
segments.s067-g44: The second 'که' resumes/explains: 'ما را' = 'to us / for us' (object/topic). 'همی' is a classical narrative particle here reinforcing immediacy/necessity ('indeed / must'), paired with 'باید' = 'it is necessary / must'. 'ای پرخرد' is a vocative address ('O very-wise one'). Grammatically this says that such an outcome must not befall us — a statement of necessity addressed to a wise listener.
segments.s067-g45: 'چنان دان' is an imperative/hortative phrase meaning 'know/consider it so' (چنان = thus, دان = know/consider). The following 'که' introduces the content clause: 'رودابه را پور سام' strings together 'رودابه را' (Rudābeh, marked with را for emphasis/definiteness) + 'پور سام' ('the son of Sām' — پور = son). The construction says: 'Consider it so, that Rudābeh's son is (the) son of Sām.'
segments.s067-g46: 'نهانی' (secretly/covertly) modifies the verb 'نهادست'. Here 'نهادست' is an older/poetic compound equivalent to 'نهاد است' or 'نهاده‌است' meaning 'has placed / has set'. 'هر گونه دام' = 'every kind of trap/snare' (هر گونه = every sort, دام = trap). The clause therefore states that 'every sort of snare has been secretly laid.'
segments.s067-g47: 'ببردست' is a compound/adverbial phrase meaning 'by hand / with a hand at hand / readily'; here it functions adverbially. 'روشن' ('clear, bright, discerning') modifies the agent (i.e. 'clear-sighted' or 'clear of purpose'). 'دلش را' = 'his heart' with object marker 'را'. 'ز راه' = 'from the way/path' (ز = from, راه = way). The sequence says that, with a ready/skillful hand and clear mind, he turned his heart away from the path (i.e. diverted his feelings or intentions).
segments.s067-g48: 'یکی' = 'one / a single', 'چاره' = 'remedy / expedient', 'مان' here is a colloquial/poetic possessive 'our' (i.e. 'چاره‌مان' = 'our remedy'); 'کرد' is past 'made/did' (he made/devised). 'باید نگاه' follows as a short clause: 'باید' = 'it is necessary / must' and 'نگاه' (= 'look/watch/heed') functions nominally, so the line concludes that he 'made for us one remedy; (we) must watch/pay heed.' Grammatically the verb 'کرد' governs 'یکی چاره مان' (he made one remedy for us) and the following 'باید نگاه' states the necessary action regarding it.
segments.s067-g33: به + noun phrase marks the thing toward which something 'turns' (verb گردیدن). شادی و انده is a coordinated noun phrase ('joy and sorrow'). نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive/optative 3rd-person singular of گردیدن here meaning 'will not turn' or 'does not turn', and دگر after the verb means 'again'/'anymore'. Word order is typical of Persian poetry: prepositional phrase + verb + discourse adverb.
segments.s067-g34: برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این 'on/against this' (classical compression). نیست is the present third-person singular 'is not/does not exist'. پیکار is the noun 'battle/contest' and با دادگر ('with the Just One') names the opponent; overall the clause means 'there is no battle against this with the Just (one)'. The order places the prepositional/locative head before the copula, a normal poetic arrangement.
segments.s067-g35: بدو is the clitic-form prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb. گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (classical Persian uses گفت for 'said'); سیندخت is the speaking agent and این داستان the direct object 'this tale'. The neutral order is 'Sindukht said this tale to him' though the poem places the indirect object first for emphasis.
segments.s067-g36: بروی دگر is a classical idiom meaning 'with a changed face/appearance' or 'toward another appearance' (بـِ روی = 'on the face, in appearance' + دگر 'another'). بر نهد is the present/past-poetic form of نهادن 'to place, to put', here 'she placed/assumed'. باستان ('ancient'/'old') qualifies what is placed or the manner; overall: 'she assumed/put on an ancient/old manner with a changed face'.
segments.s067-g37: خرد یافته is a compound participial phrase 'having acquired wisdom' (خرد 'wisdom' + یافته 'found/acquired') functioning as an epithet before the noun. موبد نیک‌بخت is the noun phrase 'the mobad (priest), fortunate' where نیک‌بخت is an epithet meaning 'fortunate'. This is a common classical order: participle/epithet + title + epithet.
segments.s067-g38: به فرزند marks the indirect object 'to/for the son'. زد is the past tense of زدن used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'related/told' (زدن + داستان = to tell a tale). داستان درخت is the direct object 'the story of the tree' (the genitive relation is expressed by simple juxtaposition in poetry).
segments.s067-g39: زدم is first-person past of زدن 'I told/related' (here used with داستان as 'I told the story'). تا introduces purpose ('so that' / 'in order that'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'by', and راه خرد literally 'the way/path of wisdom' (راه 'way' + خرد 'wisdom'). Together: 'I told the tale so that, by the way of wisdom...'.
segments.s067-g40: سپهبد is the addressee/subject 'the spahbed'. به گفتار من is a prepositional phrase 'to my speech/my words' (به + گفتار + possessive من). بنگرد is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person singular of نگریستن 'to look/consider' used here with an optative sense 'may he regard/consider'. The clause therefore means 'may the spahbed consider my words'.
segments.s067-g89: The initial phrase چِه باشد که (here written که باشد که) is an idiomatic optative/indirect-question frame meaning roughly “could it be that…”. باشد is the subjunctive/optative 'be' (here used to express doubt or possibility). پیوند سام سوار is a noun phrase 'the bond/connection of Sam the rider' — poetic Persian often omits the ezafe vowel, so پیوند (bond) + سام (Sam, possessor) + سوار (appositive 'the horseman') appear in sequence.
segments.s067-g90: نخواهد is the negative future/auxiliary 'will not' (negative of خواهد); the main verb ('reach, extend') is omitted and understood from context. ز ... تا forms a poetic from–to range (ز is the older/poetic variant of از, تا = 'as far as'). Thus ز اهواز تا قندهار = 'from Ahvaz as far as Kandahar.'
segments.s067-g91: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت here functions as 'said' (past) though the listed senses are nominal — syntactically the order is inverted (بدو گفت سیندخت rather than سیندخت بدو گفت). کای is a vocative 'O', addressing the addressee; سرفراز is an epithet 'exalted/proud' applied to the addressee.
segments.s067-g92: به گفتار کژی is a prepositional phrase 'by/with crooked speech' (به + گفتار = 'by/with speech'; کژی = 'crookedness, perverse wording'). مبادم is the negative optative/subjunctive 'may I not become / may it not be that I become' and expresses a wish; نیاز is 'need' or 'necessity'. The phrase thus means 'may I never be put in the necessity of using crooked speech.'
segments.s067-g93: گزند تو پیدا places the possessive pronoun تو after the noun (a common poetic order): 'your harm is apparent' (پیدا = 'visible/apparent'). The second half, گزند منست, is terse/elliptical; in classical poetic usage منست can function as a terse predicate and here completes the thought contrastively (rendered in the translation as a linking thought to the next hemistich).
segments.s067-g94: دل درمند تو = 'your afflicted/suffering heart' (possession marked by word order rather than an overt ezafe). بند منست: بند = 'bond, fetter'; منست is a brief poetic predicate form completing the line ('is mine / is the thing that binds'), so the clause reads 'your afflicted heart is the bond (that binds me)'.
segments.s067-g95: چنین است 'thus it is' is a fixed formula. و این بر دلم شد درست is idiomatic: بر دِلِّم شدن ('to take hold upon my heart') — شد is the past-of-become 'became', and درست here qualifies the resulting state (meaning 'firm/true' or 'fixed'). The whole clause: 'and this became firmly fixed on my heart.'
segments.s067-g96: همین بدگمانی مرا از نخست = 'this very suspicion of mine from the first.' Here همین 'this very' emphasizes بدگمانی 'mistrust/suspicion'. مرا is the first-person object/possessive clitic ('of me / my') written in the line as the separate m + را; از نخست = 'from the beginning/at first' (از marks origin/time).
segments.s067-g17: به ناکام is a prepositional phrase: به + ناکام, where ناکام ('the unsuccessful, the failed one') is used nominally. باید is the modal 'must/ought to', and سپرد is the past-stem verb 'to entrust/hand over' placed at the end in the classical verb-final order; به دشمن is the indirect object 'to the enemy'. Together the clause reads literally 'to the unsuccessful one it is necessary to hand over to the enemy' (i.e. 'one must hand the unsuccessful over to the enemy').
segments.s067-g18: همه رنج ما is a noun phrase 'all our suffering' (همه quantifier + رنج + possessive ما). باد before باید is a poetic/optative particle (an emphatic/jussive flavor) and باید + شمرد expresses necessity 'must be counted'; شمرد is the verb in final position. The whole clause = 'all our suffering must be counted.'
segments.s067-g19: یکی functions as 'one' / 'a certain' introducing the noun تنگ تابوت ('narrow coffin'). ازین (از + ین, an older spelling of این) + بهر = 'from this for' and ماست (ما + ست) is the copula 'is for us': literal order 'one narrow coffin from this for us is' (classical word order).
segments.s067-g20: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree' (the -ی ending). که is the relative 'which/that'; تریاک او = 'its teryak' (the tree's resin/sap/narcotic) with او as the possessive pronoun 'its'. زهر ماست is 'is our poison' (زهر 'poison' + ماست 'is ours/corresponds to us'). The relative clause modifies درختی: 'a tree whose sap is our poison.'
segments.s067-g21: بکشتیم is 1st plural past; in this botanical context it reads 'we planted' (ب + کشتم stem used for planting). و connects the two actions. دادیم is 'we gave', with آبش ('its water') as direct object (آب + ـش = its water/sap). به رنج is an adverbial phrase 'with toil/with difficulty' modifying دادیم.
segments.s067-g22: بیاویختیم is 1st plural past 'we hung/attached (onto it)'. از برش here is a classical locative-like phrasing (از + بر + ـش) meaning 'upon/on its surface' or 'from its branches' (the pronominal suffix ش = 'it/its'). تاج و گنج are the coordinated objects 'crown and treasure' which we hung on it. The verb-final order places the verb first and objects after in this poetic sequence.
segments.s067-g23: چو is the archaic/poetic conjunction 'when'. بر شد is a compound-like motion/becoming 'rose / was raised toward' (بر indicates upward/toward); به خورشید 'toward the sun'. و شد سایه‌دار: second شد 'became' and سایه‌دار 'shade-bearing/leafy' — together 'when it rose toward the sun and became shady.'
segments.s067-g24: به خاک اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came into the earth' (به + خاک 'to the earth', اندر 'into/in', آمد 'came')—an idiom meaning 'fell to the ground' or 'came down into the soil'. سر مایه‌دار follows as the noun phrase 'the head/chief who is wealthy' (سر 'head/top' + مایه‌دار 'wealthy'), serving as the subject that 'came down'. The classical order allows the verb-clause before the subject phrase.
segments.s067-g81: وی is the third‑person pronoun 'he/it' placed before the prepositional phrase; از گرگساران literally 'from the wolf‑lands' (از + noun). ب + دین here form the adverbial 'بدین' = 'in this way' (ب as prepositional/prefix + this), and گشت is the past/preterite meaning 'turned/became' so بدین گشت باز = 'turned back in this way' (باز = 'back/again'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: pronoun → source → manner → verb → directional adverb.
segments.s067-g82: گشاده شدست is a perfect/resultative phrase: گشاده 'open/frank' with the auxiliary/ending شدست 'has become/is made', so 'has been made open/clear'. این سخن نیست راز follows as a comment: 'this speech/statement is not a secret' (این = 'this', سخن = 'speech/statement', نیست = 'is not', راز = 'secret').
segments.s067-g83: چنین گفت is an introductory 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus'); گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' (the lemma sense list given is conversational, so treated as the verb). مهراب is the speaker's name in subject position after the verb (typical epic inversion), and کای ماه‌روی is a vocative phrase: کای = 'O', ماه‌روی = 'moon‑faced / beautiful‑faced'.
segments.s067-g84: This is the direct address content: سخن هیچ (no/any speech) + با من (to/with me) + به کژی (in crookedness / with perversity) + مگوی (negative imperative 'do not say'). Literally: 'Do not speak any crooked words to me.' Note the particle هیچ negates 'سخن' ('no speech / not a single word').
segments.s067-g85: چنین خود کی اندر خورد با خرد is a rhetorical question: چنین = 'thus', خود = 'himself', کی = 'who', اندر = 'in/within', خورد here functions in the sense 'befit / is appropriate' or 'is brought about' (an archaic/poetic use of خورد), and با خرد = 'with wisdom'. Together: 'Who, himself wise, would do/accept such a thing?' Note the inverted order (چنین ... کی ...).
segments.s067-g86: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/that which'). مر = 'to me / for me' (enclitic object), خاک را = 'the earth' with the object/definite marker را, باد is the noun 'wind' here, and فرمان برد is a verbal phrase 'to take/receive command' (i.e. 'obey' or 'be commanded'). Grammatically this clause gives the astonishing content: 'that the earth should obey the wind toward me.'
segments.s067-g87: This clause states a condition of feeling: مرا دل (literally 'to me, the heart' = 'my heart') + بدین = 'by this / in this' (ب + این) + نیستی/نیست (negative copula 'is not') + دردمند = 'sorrowful/afflicted'. In other words: 'My heart is not sorrowful about this.' The compact word order (possessive before noun, prepositional phrase before copula) is typical of classical Persian verse.
segments.s067-g88: اگر introduces the proviso 'if / provided that'. ایمنی یابمی is an optative/formal subjunctive: ایمنی = 'safety/security', یابمی = 'may he/one find' (i.e. 'I may obtain' or 'one might obtain'). از گزند means 'from harm/damage.' Together: 'if I may obtain safety from harm.'
segments.s067-g49: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'plenty' modifying دادمش ('I gave him'); دادمش is a past verb with the enclitic -مش ('to him') attached, so the clause reads 'I gave him much advice.' The conjunction و links to سودش نکرد, an impersonal/idiomatic clause where سود (benefit) takes the possessive clitic -ش and نکرد is the negated past of کردن: 'it did him no good.'
segments.s067-g50: دلش ('his heart') is a noun plus the possessive clitic -ش; خیره is a predicative adjective 'astonished/stunned' and the copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian). بینم is first-person present ('I see') used by the narrator to report what he observes. همی is an adverb giving continuity ('still/ever') and modifies روی زرد ('a pale face'—روی 'face', زرد 'pale/wan'), so the sequence reads 'I see his heart stunned and his face still pale.'
segments.s067-g51: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'; مهراب is the subject. The phrase بر پای جست is an idiom 'leapt to his feet' with بر indicating direction/toward and جست the past of 'to leap.' Word order places the verb after the phrase, typical in epic/past narrative.
segments.s067-g52: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' and is the main verb here; the following prepositional/archaic locative از بر + noun expresses the place/position ('upon/on'); دست شمشیر is a compound/genitive construction meaning 'hand of the sword' (i.e. the hilt). Together: 'he placed his hand on the sword.'
segments.s067-g53: تنش ('his body') + گشت ('became') forms 'his body became ...' with the predicate adjective لرزان ('trembling'). The coordinating و links to رخ لاجورد, an elliptical nominal phrase meaning '(his) face (became) lapis-blue'—classical Persian often omits the copula or repeats it elliptically.
segments.s067-g54: پر از X is a repeated pattern 'full of X.' پر از خون جگر means 'filled with the blood of the jigar' (جگر here used figuratively as the seat of feeling), and دل پر از باد سرد uses the same pattern 'the heart full of cold wind' (باد سرد as 'cold wind'—a poetic way to convey chill/shock). Each noun phrase is linked by parallel structure.
segments.s067-g55: همی گفت indicates repeated/ongoing speech ('she kept saying'); رودابه را uses the object marker را after the proper name to mark the addressee ('to Rudabeh'). رود خون is the noun phrase uttered as direct speech ('a river of blood'). The clause thus reports continuous speech addressed to Rudabeh followed by the quoted phrase.
segments.s067-g56: بروی زمین is an infinitival/verb phrase built on رفت/رو meaning 'to go/flow upon the ground'; بر کنم is first-person present/future 'I will cause/make (it so)' with کنم as the finite verb. هم کنون is an adverbial phrase 'this very moment/right now.' Together they form the speech-act 'I will make it flow on the ground immediately.'
segments.s067-g65: مرا گفت is a reporting phrase: مرا (to me) + گفت (said), with the subject omitted (he/they said). چون ... آمد پدید is a temporal clause: چون (as/when) + دختر آمد (the maiden came) + پدید (appeared/was revealed) — the two verbs آمد پدید act together as a poetic doublet meaning 'came forth / appeared'.
segments.s067-g66: ببایستش is an impersonal/modal construction meaning 'it must / it is necessary' with the enclitic ـش = 'her' (i.e. 'it must be done to her'). اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time / then'. سر برید is a terse verbal phrase 'cut (off) the head' where برید is the past-stem used as the action, and the object ('her') is carried by the enclitic in ببایستش.
segments.s067-g67: This line uses a sequence of past actions in terse, archaic diction. نکشتم appears as a negated first‑person past ('I did not ...') though the exact root is ambiguous in the lexicon given; بگشتم is an archaic past form meaning 'I returned' or 'I turned back' (cf. برگشتم). ز راه = 'from/off the road'. نیا here reads as an imperative/negative 'do not come' (or could be the vocative/marker in other contexts), but in sequence it functions as an instruction/negation following the reported actions.
segments.s067-g68: کنون is the adverb 'now'. ساخت is the past 'he/it made' or 'he contrived'. بر من here means 'against me / upon me' (a directional/targetive use). چنین کیمیا = 'such an alchemy / such a contrivance' where کیمیا is used metaphorically for a contrived device or elixir.
segments.s067-g69: پسر ... کو is an archaic relative construction: پسر (son) + کو (who/that) introduces the relative clause. ز راه پدر = 'from/away from the father's road/way'. بگذرد is third-person singular present/subjunctive 'passes / departs / surpasses' (here 'depart from / go beyond').
segments.s067-g70: دلیرش is دلیر (brave) with the possessive/enclitic ـش = 'his bravery' or 'his being brave'. ز پشت پدر is a poetical idiom 'from (behind) the father's back' meaning 'by virtue of the father's protection/support'. نشمرد is a prohibitive/jussive negative of شمردن: 'do not count / do not consider'. The clause therefore says one should not attribute the son's courage to his father's support.
segments.s067-g71: همم بیم جانست is compact: همم here expresses the speaker's concern/feeling, بیم = 'fear', and جانست is an archaic copular phrase literally 'is for life' — together 'my concern is fear for my life'. و هم جای ننگ adds 'and likewise (it is) a place of shame' — i.e. 'and also (I fear) dishonor / shame'.
segments.s067-g72: چرا is 'why'. بازداری is either the present you‑form 'you restrain / keep back' or the noun 'restraint'; here it functions as a verbal action 'do you keep back'. سرم را = 'my head' with object marker را (the lexicon given doesn't list 'head' for سر in that entry, so the pronoun sense is indicated in the senses map below). ز جنگ = 'from battle/war'. The whole line asks 'Why do you keep my head (i.e. keep me) from battle?'
segments.s067-g97: "اگر" introduces a conditional ('if'); "باشد" is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of شدن/بودن used here to mean 'it be / it is the case'; "این نیست کاری شگفت" literally 'this is not a wondrous/strange thing' — "کاری" (here 'a thing/act') is the head noun followed by the adjective "شگفت" ('strange/wonderful'). Word order is typical poetic Persian: condition + comment.
segments.s067-g98: "که" links the remark to the following clause ('that'); "چندین" means 'several/many'; "بد اندیشه" is a compound meaning 'evil/ill thoughts' or 'suspicion'; "باید گرفت" is a modal construction: "باید" = 'one must/it is necessary' plus the infinitive/verb "گرفت" ('to take/ to seize' — idiomatically 'to entertain/take up' thoughts).
segments.s067-g99: "فریدون" is the sentence subject (proper name). The phrase "به سرو یمن" is a poetic epithet ('as/like the cypress of Yemen') placed before the verb; "گشت" is past of گشتن 'became/turned (into)' and "شاه" is the predicate noun 'king' — together 'Fereydun became king, a cypress of Yemen' (epithet praising his stature/beauty).
segments.s067-g100: "جهانجوی" is an epithet 'world‑seeking/ambitious' that qualifies the king (it belongs with "شاه"); "دستان" here is literal 'hands' (instrumental/poetic usage) or a brief apposition; "همین دید راه" = 'he saw this very road/path' — "دید" is past 'saw' and "راه" is the object 'way/path'. The phrase as a whole states that the ambitious king perceived this particular path.
segments.s067-g101: "هرانگه که" is a temporal subordinating phrase 'whenever/at the moment that'; "بیگانه شد" is 'became a stranger/grew estranged' (شد = became); "خویش تو" = 'your own disposition/habit' — in poetic word order the possessive is juxtaposed rather than linked by an izafe, so this reads 'when he becomes a stranger, your own nature...' (i.e. the addressee's temperament).
segments.s067-g102: "شود" is future/infra‑past 'will become' (from شدن); "تیره رای" lit. 'dark opinion' = 'gloomy/ominous counsel' ("تیره" modifying "رای"); "بداندیش تو" = 'you of ill‑thought' i.e. 'you will be ill‑disposed/ill‑thinking' — here "تو" addresses the addressee. The clause predicts a darkening of mind and ill counsel.
segments.s067-g103: "به سیندخت فرمود" = 'he said/commanded to Sindukht' where "به" marks the indirect object and "فرمود" is the past polite/royal 'he said/ordered'; "پس نامدار" is an appositive/epithet 'then/afterwards, the renowned' describing Sindukht. Word order places the epithet after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s067-g104: "که" introduces the reported command ('that' / introducing direct speech); "رودابه را" is the definite object 'Rudabeh' marked by the object particle "را"; "خیز" is an imperative 'rise! / get up!' used here as part of the instruction to bring her forward; "پیش من" = 'before me / in my presence'; "آر" is an imperative 'bring' (archaic/poetic) completing the command 'bring Rudabeh before me.'
segments.s067-g9: ازین is composed of از + این and functions as a prepositional/demonstrative phrase 'from/of this' (here translated as 'this'). کاخ (noun) is followed by the adjective آباد (adjective after noun, as is normal in Persian) giving 'prosperous palace.' The coordinating و links a parallel noun phrase این خواسته ('this desired/chosen [dwelling]'); the phrase is elliptical (no copula) and both noun phrases serve as the theme of the following clause.
segments.s067-g10: وزین is an attributive adjective meaning 'stately/dignified' modifying the following noun phrase تازی اسپان ('Arabian horses'); note the poetic placement of adjectives before the noun-phrase. آراسته is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned/arrayed' that comes after the noun-phrase and predicates that the horses adorn the preceding places.
segments.s067-g11: وزین is an adjective 'stately/dignified' modifying بندگان; بندگان here means 'followers/subjects' rather than only 'slaves.' سپهبدپرست is a compound adjective/noun (سپَهبد + پرست) meaning 'pro‑spahbed' or 'those who honor/revere the general' and attaches to بندگان, forming a single descriptive noun phrase.
segments.s067-g12: ازین (از + این) again functions demonstratively 'this.' تاج و این خسروانی are coordinated nouns 'crown and this kingship/sovereignty' where این specifies the second noun. نشست is used as a past action 'sat / took their seat' (verb); the clause is elliptical about the agent (the previously mentioned بندگان).
segments.s067-g13: وزین again 'stately/dignified' modifies the coordinated nouns چهره ('countenance/appearance') and سرو بالای ما. سرو here is used metaphorically for a tall/lofty figure ('tall beauty'); بالای ما is a possessive phrase 'of/for us' (literally 'the height above of us'), so the whole phrase means 'our lofty/cypress-like stature.' Word order and imagery are poetic.
segments.s067-g14: وزین modifies نام ('name') giving 'illustrious name/reputation.' The conjunction و links this to این دانش و رای ما — 'this learning and counsel of ours.' این is a demonstrative pointing to the immediately mentioned qualities; رای here is best read as 'counsel / plan' rather than merely 'vote.'
segments.s067-g15: بدین is poetic contraction of به + این and functions as 'by/with this.' آبداری is an abstract noun meaning 'hospitality / the serving of refreshments' (a traditional form of generous service). The coordinating و links this to این راستی ('this uprightness/rectitude'); both are instrumental circumstances for what follows.
segments.s067-g16: زمان تا زمان is an iterative temporal phrase 'time after time / from era to era.' آورد is the past verb 'brought / caused' (here 'brought about'), and کاستی is the direct object 'shortcoming / decline' — so the clause says that repeatedly over time a deficit/decay was produced.
segments.s067-g137: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a 3rd-person past tense verb 'heard' with the subject رودابه (proper name) following it — classical Persian often places verb before subject; آن گفت‌وگوی is the object phrase 'that conversation' (آن = 'that', گفت‌وگوی = 'conversation' in construct/definite form).
segments.s067-g138: دژم گشت is a simple past copular construction meaning 'became sullen' (دژم = 'sullen', گشت = 'became/turned'). و = 'and' links the clauses. چون here means 'like/as', introducing a simile; زعفران کرد روی is idiomatic: کرد ('made/did') with روی ('face') means 'she made her face (a certain color)', so 'زعفران' here is the saffron color (not the spice) — 'her face took on a saffron hue'. Note the possessive or pronoun on 'روی' is omitted, a common poetic compression.
segments.s067-g139: سیه مژه is a compound noun 'black/dark lashes' (سیه + مژه). بر is the preposition 'on/over'. نرگسان is a poetic plural/metaphor (narcissi) used for beautiful eyes — 'over her narcissus-like eyes'. دژم follows and functions as a descriptive adjective 'sullen' characterizing the look; adjective placement post-nominal is common in classical Persian.
segments.s067-g140: فرو is an adverbial prefix here meaning 'deeply/downwards'. خوابنید is an archaic/poetic past of 'to sleep' (she slept/fell asleep). و links to the following adverbial expression. نزد هیچ دم is a temporal/adverbial phrase literally 'near/no moment' — 'not for a single moment' — a compact, somewhat elliptical classical expression (the exact negation can be realized implicitly in neighboring lines in poetry).
segments.s067-g141: پدر is the subject 'the father'. This line contains two parallel noun+adjective constructions: دل پر از خشم = 'a heart full of anger' (دل + پر 'full' + از + خشم) and سر پر ز جنگ = 'a head full of battle/war' (ز is the poetic variant of از). Note پر follows the noun and combines with از/ز to mean 'filled with'.
segments.s067-g142: همی رفت is the archaic/progressive construction meaning 'was going' or 'went on' (همی gives iterative/continuous aspect). غران is a present-participle/adverbial adjective 'roaring', modifying the motion, and بسان پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard' placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s067-g143: سوی خانه شد is a idiomatic intransitive phrasing 'turned/took herself toward the house' — شد here denotes 'turned/moved' (classical usage). دختر دل‌شده is a noun phrase 'the daughter/maiden smitten in heart' (دل‌شده = 'love-struck' or 'distraught'), with the past-participle used adjectivally after the noun.
segments.s067-g144: This short string is a stack of epithets describing her appearance/manner: رخان (loosely 'loose/relaxed', here implying loosened hair or slackened features), معصفر 'yellowish/pale', بزر 'great/very' (intensifier or 'much'), and آژده 'startled/terrified'. Classical Persian often lists such adjectives in sequence without explicit conjunctions.
segments.s067-g25: This is a copular noun phrase: برینست is a fused/poetic copula (برِ این است / 'it is thus') placing focus on what follows. فرجام و انجام ما is a coordinated noun phrase 'the end and result of us' where the possessor ما follows the nouns (post-nominal possessive), so the whole segment reads roughly 'such is our end and outcome.'
segments.s067-g26: بدان is b‑ + دان, an imperative/jussive 'know / consider'. It introduces a subordinate interrogative/extent clause marked by تا + کجا ('to what point / how far'). باشد is a 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative copula here ('may be / is'), and آرام ما is the noun 'our tranquillity/peace' with the possessive ما after the noun.
segments.s067-g27: به marks the indirect object/recipient ('to Sindukht'). The sequence مُهراب گفت is the agent + past verb 'Mehrob said', and این سخن is the direct object 'these words / this speech'. Word order here is topicalizer (recipient) + subject + past verb + object.
segments.s067-g28: نوآوردی is a noun meaning 'novelty / innovation' used as the predicate or comment. و connects the clauses. نو نگردد کهن is 'the new does not become old': نگردد is a present/subjunctive/epistolary form 'become / turn into' with negative sense here, and کهن is the adjective 'old/ancient' functioning as predicate.
segments.s067-g29: سرای سپنجی is a noun phrase (head noun + epithet/attributive), here the subject 'Sepanj's house' (proper name + سرای). بدین سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus' and is placed before the copula بود (past 'was'), so the clause means 'The house of Sepanj was like this.'
segments.s067-g30: خرد یافته is a compound where یافته is a past participle 'having acquired' modifying خرد 'wisdom' — together 'having acquired wisdom' or 'the wise (one)'. زو is the poetic contraction of ز + او = 'from him/her' (zō). هراسان بود is the predicate 'was frightened', with بود as past copula. The sequence therefore reads 'the wise one, having acquired [knowledge] from him, was frightened.'
segments.s067-g31: This contains two short clauses in parataxis: یکی اندر آید 'one/ someone comes in' (یکی = 'one/someone', اندر = 'in/into', آید = present/narrative 'comes') and دگر بگذرد 'another passes' (دگر = 'another', elided noun implied, بگذرد = 'passes'). The verbs are in the narrative/presentive used in storytelling.
segments.s067-g32: گذر نی is an elliptical negative nominal clause 'there is no passing' (گذر = 'passage/passing', نی = poetic negative 'not'). که introduces the explanatory/cause clause 'for/that' and چرخش is 'the turning / the Wheel' (often fate). همی بسپرد is an older verbal form (همی + بسپرد) meaning 'will consign/hand over' (present/future sense). Together: 'there is no passing, for the Wheel will hand them over.'
segments.s067-g113: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. ماند is the past 3rd-person singular of ماندن 'to remain/ be left' and carries the main verb meaning here. The two coordinated noun phrases بر و بوم and مام و باب are the things that 'did not remain' ( objects of ماند). This word order (verb then coordinated nouns) is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s067-g114: شود is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form of شدن 'to become' used here as a narrative/predicative verb 'becomes/was made'. پست is the predicate adjective 'low, abased' that follows شود. رودابه is a proper name (the subject/understood topic of this clause). با + noun expresses accompaniment ('with/along with the stream').
segments.s067-g115: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past 3rd-person singular 'heard'. The following noun phrase سیندخت is the subject. سر پیش اوی places 'head' (direct object) 'before him/in his presence'; اوی is an archaic/enclitic form of the third‑person pronoun used here to mark the person in front of whom the action happens.
segments.s067-g116: فرو برد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'to put down / lower (deeply)' where فرو (down) combines with برد (past of بردن) to give the verbal sense 'bowed / lowered'. The coordinating و links the next action: بر خاک بنهاد روی literally 'placed/laid (her) face upon the earth' — بنهاد is a past-form verb 'placed/put', بر خاک is prepositional 'on the earth', and روی is the noun 'face' (the thing placed).
segments.s067-g117: بر دختر آمد is an inverted, poetic order equivalent to 'a (something) came upon the maiden' (literally 'upon the maiden came'). آمد is the past 'came'. The following nominal phrase پر از خنده لب describes what came: لب 'lip' full (پر) 'of laughter' (از خنده) — the modifier sequence is typical of classical Persian where the 'full of X' phrase follows the noun it modifies.
segments.s067-g118: گشاده رخ روزگون is a compact adjectival description: گشاده 'open/bright' modifies رخ 'face', and روزگون 'rosy' further qualifies it. زیر شب is a prepositional phrase 'under the night / beneath the darkness' that situates the scene poetically. Word order (adjectives following the noun or appearing in sequence) is typical of Shahnameh's elevated style.
segments.s067-g119: همی is an adverb often signaling continuous or habitual action ('kept on / continually'). مژده دادش is 'gave her the good tidings' — دادش is داد + enclitic ش marking the indirect object 'to her' (past 3rd‑person gave). که introduces the subordinate clause that reports the content of the tidings. جنگی is an adjectival epithet 'warlike' modifying پلنگ 'leopard'.
segments.s067-g120: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گور is 'grave' and ژیان 'life' — together the phrase z gūr-e zhiyān (here in terse poetic order) indicates source or object related to life and the grave. کرد is the past 3rd‑person of کردن 'did/made' and carries the main action in this clause. کوتاه چنگ is an adjectival/adverbial phrase: کوتاه 'short/brief' modifies چنگ 'claw/strike' used metaphorically as a quick, cutting stroke. The whole cluster says that life was cut/shortened (metaphorically by a claw) coming from the grave.
segments.s067-g121: This is an imperative sequence. کنون زود places the action immediately and quickly (adverbials before the verb). پیرایه بگشای is the imperative 'undo/remove the ornament' (بگشای = open/remove). و رو is a short command ‘and show your face / turn’ where رو functions as ‘face/turn (toward)’, linked to the following prepositional phrase (رو به ...).
segments.s067-g122: به پیش پدر شو is another imperative clause: به پیش = 'to/in the presence of', پدر = 'father', شو = imperative of شدن 'be/come' (i.e. 'present yourself'). به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'with lamentation' (به used adverbially), and بنو at the end is unclear here (looks like a name/clan element in the supplied lexicon) and cannot be matched to a normal verb sense in the provided senses.
segments.s067-g123: بدو گفت رودابه is a reporting clause 'Rudabeh said to him' where بدو = 'to him/unto him' (prepositional pronoun). گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' (the supplied senses listed a noun; functionally it is the verb گفت). پیرایه چیست is a direct question 'what is the ornament?' with چی = 'what' and ست (is) as the copula.
segments.s067-g124: به جای is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in place of / instead of' (به + جای). سر مایه literally 'head-substance' = 'head’s adornment/treasure' (سر = head, مایه = substance/resource). بی‌مایه چیست is the question 'what is that without substance / worthless thing?' (بی‌مایه = 'worthless'). The final ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s067-g125: This clause asserts a relationship: روان = 'soul/spirit'; the following elements express possession/identification (the older syntax allows روان مرا for 'my soul'). پور سام = 'son of Sam' (پور = son/descendant, سام a proper name). م (given as the copula in the supplied tokens) functions here as the copula 'is' linking the parts, and جفت = 'mate/spouse' means 'match' or 'suitable pair.' Overall: 'My soul's match is the son of Sam.'
segments.s067-g126: چرا آشکارا بباید نهفت is a rhetorical question. چرا = 'why', آشکارا = 'openly/evidently' (adverb), بباید = modal 'ought/must' (present/future sense 'should'), and نهفت = passive/ adjectival form 'be hidden' or 'kept concealed'. Together: 'Why should it be kept hidden openly?' (i.e. why hide it?).
segments.s067-g127: به پیش پدر شد is a verbal clause: به پیش = 'before / in the presence of', پدر = 'father', شد = past of شدن 'became / stood' (here 'came/appeared before'). چو خورشید شرق is a simile: چو = 'like/as', خورشید = 'sun', شرق = 'east' ('like the sun of the east' = brilliant like the eastern sun).
segments.s067-g128: به یاقوت و زر اندرون گشته غرق describes her adornment: به یاقوت و زر = 'in/with ruby and gold', اندرون = 'within/inside', گشته is a perfect/participial form of شدن 'has become / is turned', and غرق here means 'immersed/overwhelmed' (i.e. 'immersed in ruby and gold' — richly adorned).
